content: In traditional finance, central banks serve as trusted intermediaries that validate and record transactions, maintaining a single authoritative ledger. Blockchain circumvents this central authority by distributing the ledger across a decentralized network of nodes, each maintaining a synchronized copy. Transactions are cryptographically secured using digital signatures, ensuring authenticity and non-repudiation. The ledger’s immutability is enforced through cryptographic hash functions linking blocks of transactions, rendering retrospective alterations computationally prohibitive. Crucially, consensus algorithms such as Proof of Work or Proof of Stake enable the network to agree on the transaction order and ledger state in a Byzantine fault-tolerant manner, mitigating risks of double-spending and censorship without relying on centralized trust. Incentive mechanisms align participant behavior, fostering honest validation and propagation of transactions. Together, these components form a robust, trustless infrastructure whereby transaction records emerge from collective agreement and cryptographic guarantees rather than centralized oversight, redefining the paradigm of transactional trust and ledger integrity.
